speaker: interviewer
text: October , 1995 at Patricia Stein, interviewing Irene Hirschfeld, born Schek, in Delray Beach, Florida, in the United States. Tape one is in English. Today's the 9th of October 1995. I'm Patricia Stein here to interview Irene Hirschfeld, born Schek, in Delray Beach, Florida, United States. The tape is in English, and this is tape number one. Good afternoon, Mrs. Hirschfeld. Could you give me your name, your name at birth, your-- if you had a nickname, your birth date, and your city and country of birth, and how old you are today?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Irene Schek, S-C-H-E-K. I was born in Cracow, Poland, in 1921. Now I live in Florida, Delray Beach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you grow in Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I went to schools there, first the grammar school, and then junior high, and high school. And the war started, of course, my education ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived in your town.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a suburb of Cracow where I lived. So it wasn't as large and as populist as the Cracow itself. But I would say that a few thousand must have lived there too, just the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it-- what is it called, the suberb that--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many Jewish people lived there in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a lot of Jewish people, more religious and less religious, and reformed. Plenty of synagogues. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On of the subjects in school was the Jewish religion, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all the Jews live together in one area in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not until they formed a ghetto. Up till that time, everybody was free to move wherever they wanted and [INAUDIBLE] in Cracow for that matter too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all your family and your extended family lived near each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did most of the Jewish people earn their living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them were educated people. One of my uncles was a doctor. Another one was a professor. And their children were educated also. So and some have stars. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They all earned a decent living.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were in your-- were in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just two, my brother and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: George, [? Yese ?] in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: George--

speaker: interviewee
text: Schek.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Leopold. And my mother's name was [? Sarah. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Opperchingski, ?] you will never be able to spell.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was her maiden name, [? Opperchingski ?]. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then she was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sheck, after her husband. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents earn their living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother didn't work until my father passed away, of course. Very few women, Jewish women, worked. My father had a factory. And they produced bathroom accessories, chrome-covered, very beautiful, hangers for towels, and for the tooth cups, and also for the foyer, hangers for coats. They were also made out of the-- the same metal and finished with chrome, very shiny, beautiful. So this was how he earned his living, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I finished high school, and the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how-- who is-- was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. What you would call, they were already reformed, because we didn't have two different sets of dishes. And we observed the high holidays. But my parents didn't go regularity to the temple, the synagogue, on every Friday. They didn't, only on high holidays. My mother never wore a wig. So we were what you would call the reformed Jews already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you observe Pesach? Yes. We had a different set of dishes. And matzah was stored in a safe, dry place, and plenty of hard boiled eggs. How-- how were your lives affected, you know, with-- with what was happening in Germany? Did you hear anything? Did you experience any acts of anti-semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point, no. But we heard on the radio what was happening in Germany, and what was happening in-- in part of Poland, Silesia, that was on the border of Germany already. But, you know, you heard. And you didn't want to believe that this can really happen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it did happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- were there any acts of anti-semitism that you experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. There were only five Jewish girls in the classroom in the high school where I was going. And some of them were nice, and others were anti-semites. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do? What kind of stuff?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would make some comments, or you all stink, or you all-- I think it was purely jealousy because the Jewish girls were always smarter than the others. They had better grades. And they were dressed decently. And the-- the Polish people were not so very, very-- how should I say-- aristocratic. There was a part, of-- of course, that studied at the university. And they were doctors, Polish doctors, and lawyers, and so on. But the majority was not very well-educated-- very simple people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever talk about leaving the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but then we didn't have a way to go. I just remembered that my brother got from a distant-- distant relative of our father an affidavit to come to United States that he never used anyway. But this was the only meaning of getting out of Poland. Some people did, the ones that had the good fortune of buying the papers of other countries, you know, and they escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to a youth organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was a student organization when I was in high school, all through the high school. Of course, it was not legal. But everybody belonged anyway. And for a very--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: --short time, I belonged to a Zionist organization, very short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Akiba.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they prepared people to-- to go to Israel. They had courses of-- in Hebrew, and had Hebrew songs, and on like Shabbat we had. But I didn't belong for a very long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- were they involved with any kind of resistance or underground movement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not till the ghetto. Yes. When we were finally in the ghetto, they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear what was happening in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but again, the same thing. We didn't believe that this-- this can happen. We thought it's a propaganda. Somebody's just feeding information that are not true. But it was true.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happens when-- when Hitler came to power and Hitler invaded Poland? What happened in your town? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you remember the day? Every Polish-- every Polish officer and-- and the top brass ran away. This was the first. And the poor guys, the poor peasant that had the horse and buggy, this was the one that-- that remained. All went out of Poland to-- to Hungary, or whenever they could manage to smuggle themselves out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the day that the Nazis entered Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in September. But I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the day, like what happened? Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was home. I was home. And I heard the first bomb flying. And then I realized that the war started. I was very unhappy, very upset.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your lives change from this?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, the schools stop existing. So this was the first immediate change. And then the food was not the available. Sometimes you would have to go early in the morning to get the bread because there was nothing left right from the beginning. And then, of course, it got worse and worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your business still running at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a little while, yes. But my father was dead already then. And my mother was running the-- the business with little help from my brother that really didn't live in-- in Cracow. He lived and in Warsaw. But when the war started, he came to Cracow to be with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the Nazi occupation as bad for the non-Jews as it was for the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never as bad for non-Jews as it was for the Jews. The Jews were right away, you know, singled out for punishment. They used to grab Jews, Orthodox Jews that had big beards, and cut it with huge scissors, of course, not only the beard, but-- but the flesh with it too. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened to your business?

speaker: interviewee
text: It stopped existing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this because they confiscated it or just because people couldn't afford.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no-- no material to-- to work with. And the Polish people that work originally didn't want to stay anymore. And they tried to become big entrepreneur that they were never measure up to be. But they tried. You know, they have no-- no--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what were the Nazis doing? They came in. And then what did they do in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they started with certain rules. You were not supposed to be on the street after dark. And we had to wear an armband with the Magen David, star of-- of David on it. And this was the beginning, really. It's how they started, and a little by little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take way people's businesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did they do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the people that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was still the Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] get mind to how do you say it in English-- organization that was taking care of people that really had no means of supporting themselves. So they were distributing food. And then they-- I remember that I work in-- in-- one of the parts of the-- they call it the Jewish [? Gamina. ?] They moved some people from other towns to Cracow. And we were supposed to find apartments for them. And so this is what we were doing also, looking, going half a day from one street to another and asking how many people in that apartment, whether we can move another couple or a few children with-- with family that was living there. So this is what we were doing when the war started, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the non-Jews help the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much, to be honest. No. Sometimes they-- they would-- just pointed out if the Jewish woman was hiding someplace, or she might, the Polish woman, peasant, go out and scream that the Jewish girl was hiding in-- in-- in her building someplace. They were not very helpful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they told the Germans that the Jews were hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and the Polish police. The Polish policeman was no different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see anything like that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. When I was in ghetto already, I heard the Polish woman screaming, a Jewish woman worked in here! She just wanted to attract attention, somebody should follow her into the building. But there were some that did hide the-- the Jewish, mostly the girls, even in the building next door to the one that-- that I lived. She was a concierge, that woman. And I knew that she was hiding Jewish young girls in her apartment. There were very, very few. They were very eager to grab what was left from Jewish homes and Jewish businesses. And-- and they used to lie. They used to say that the German took it away from them. But they were the one that really disposed of many, many things.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after the Germans entered Cracow did they make it into a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not right away. It took a few months, really. Before that, they just let people that they thought they would be using for certain reasons. And the rest was supposed to go the suburbs. So I went with my mother to suburbs, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little suburb. It was called [? Borakfalinski ?], a little, little-- I couldn't even call it a town, because it was not a town. And this is where we stayed. My mother was still going here and there to the business to see whether she can sell anything. And I learned to cook. I became a cook in the family. I used to make potato soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you living with?

speaker: interviewee
text: We rented a-- a room from a Polish peasant family. And this is where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My brother was already in ghetto. When they make-- formed the ghetto, he right away went in. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't see him too often until we moved to ghetto also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what made you move to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us. They-- we were ordered to get out from those little towns around the Cracow and go to ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you went back into the ghetto, when you went back into Cracow, what did you find? Did you go back to where you had been-- had grown up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, because somebody else was already living in our apartment. But we moved with my mother's sister. My uncle was a doctor. And they had a big apartment. And I remember my mother and my sister were sleeping in his waiting room. But it was good because we were not alone. We had have our very close relatives with us. I adore my mother's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they form what they called the [GERMAN], place where Jews used to report in the morning. And we were being sent to unload coal from a huge truck or wash dishes someplace. The German used to employ us at the beginning in that fashion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they pay you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, of course not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what date was this from? What date did you go back into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no other way to go. There was no where to go. I-- I had an armband on my arm. And nobody would let me in. There was no other way just go back to ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So which date was this? When-- when-- what year was this that you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was right away after maybe a year later than when the Germans came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was like, 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the fall, because I remember the Jewish holidays. And they're usually fall.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in the ghetto for the Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you first went into the ghetto? What was your first memory, I mean, your impression of when you went in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was scared, absolutely scared. I remember that huge gate to the ghetto, and the-- the German soldier that was standing at the door. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he say anything to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once you passed in, you couldn't come out again. Was it closed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Some people did go that wanted. The German employed, they had special passes. And they could go out. And also, when somebody died, of course, they carried a body to the cemetery. And I remember my mother passed away in ghetto. And she was very well-liked. And a lot of people followed her to the cemetery. And the Germans decided that this was the last time they letting the Jews out for that purpose, because there are many soldiers that are being-- German soldiers, being killed. And nobody follows them to the grave. And here, one Jewish woman, and the whole ghetto practically walked after. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it soon after you went back in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a year later, maybe, a year and a half later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you join any organization when you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, always I remember there were people buying something and others selling. And one day we didn't have enough money to buy the food. So my brother said, you better sell something. And a woman showed up. [LAUGHS] And he bought a big, huge down comforter from-- from my apartment. So I had money to buy food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your uncle working during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go when he was seeing his patients?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the other room. The apartment was big. And-- but there were plenty of people in that big apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you all related?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only through my aunt, my aunt that was my mother's sister. And of course, her children were my cousins. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We tried to be together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I do all day? I was working. I was working in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a factory where they were making uniforms, German uniforms. And this was one of my jobs. And another one was-- I remember where they were making cardboard boxes also for Germans. And-- but nobody paid us for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of these-- these factories? The Jewish people, yes. Where they Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- did-- were they helpful?

speaker: interviewee
text: My experience is yes, because during one action like this, really a-- a moment will decide whether you will be alive or dead, I noticed a Jewish policeman that I knew from Cracow. And I said to him, help me. And when the German that was selecting people turned around, he pushed me to the good side. So he saved my life, really, not ashamed that he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walter Goldberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Berg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Berg. Goldberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Goldberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [INAUDIBLE], was his name, like Mr. Page. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- did-- did he save anybody else that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But I can tell you that he did save me. That's for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was not alive anymore. And my brother went out. And he came back also after the selection. So the family was shrinking. But we still were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- were there a lot of actions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, almost every week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe one?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was panic. It was-- it was really-- because we knew that when I was working already in the factory making the German uniforms, we used to come home in the evening. And we saw already German soldiers standing all around the ghetto. We knew that the action was going to take place next day. And it sure did. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what happened? Did you have to go outside during this action? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They-- they were standing in rows. And they were looking at your face. If he likes you enough, so he push you on the good side. If not, on the bad side. And this is when the Walter Goldberg came in-- in and he helped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they-- how did they find the Jewish police? How did they select them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, there were some Jewish people among us that were very, very easy to be persuaded to be Jewish policeman, and not always behaved the right way. So they are the ones directing Germans where to go and where to find Jews that were hiding, sorry to admit it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was at the beginning, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they-- did they help [? different ?] people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their role was very minimal. They really had very little to do. They maybe had a little bread to distribute to people. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, not very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they organize like entertainment or that kind of thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We did it, even among ourselves we [? would ?] sing songs. But there was no entertainment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have schools for the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Schools were closed right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the shuls-- what happened with the shuls? Did people have--

speaker: interviewee
text: The shuls were existing. But it wasn't a very safe place to be in the shuls, because Germans know where to find Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They're very religious, were going, of course. They were praying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a small underground, mostly young people that belonged to a Zionist organization before the war. The Akiba, I think I mentioned to you. This was the only underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so they managed to achieve anything that you knew of?

speaker: interviewee
text: They smuggled some people, yes, through the sewers outside the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Nazis know that they were in existence?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so, no. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Nazis respond? Like, if-- if there was an act of resistance, if you tried to-- to resist them in some way, not follow the orders, how did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they will shoot you right then and then on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anything like that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to hit you. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did the-- the actions go on for? Did they start immediately that they-- they formed the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. Yeah, pretty soon after. Yeah. And the first day, of course, took the old people. And I remember standing during one action. And I said to myself, I have no more problems because there's nobody older than myself that's living anymore in-- in the building where I live. My mother was dead. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
